Farn Polrrrcrl Pnncucss CovrlrrssroN
P.O. Box ll07 . llli J Street . Slcramento. CA f-itill-0tt07
t9l6) 112-5660 . Fax t9l6) 321-0tlti6
August 29,2000
Patricia S. Howe
City Clerk, Redwood City
1017 Middlefield Road
Redwood City, Califomia 9 4064-03 9 I
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-148
Dear Ms. Howe:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Redwood City
Councilmember Jeffrey Ira regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act ("Act").I
QUESTION
Councilmember Ira has a client who owns a corrmercial building across the street
from the site of a proposed theatre complex. As a member of the city's redevelopment
agency, does he have a conflict-of-interest in the decision to approve the project?
CONCLUSION
Yes. The decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial on
commercial property owned by Councilmember Ira's client.
FACTS2
Councilmember Ira is an accountant and business partner in an accounting firm
known as C.G. Uhlenberg & Company,LLC. As a partner, Councilmember Ira has more
than a 10 percent interest in the accounting firm. One of his clients, a small partnership,
owns and leases a cofilmercial building in the city's redevelopment area. The
I See Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear atTitle 2,
Sections 18109-18996 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 The facts in this letter come from your request for advice and subsequent telephone
conversations with Councilmember Ira.


Our File No. A-00-148
Page No. 2
commercial building is across the street from the site of a proposed theatre complex,
which is also in the redevelopment area.
Sitting as the city's redevelopment agency, the city council will be deliberating
and voting on whether to approve the proposed theatre. Councilmember Ira believes ihat
the decision may affect the fair market value of his client's property by $10,000 or more.
The city has five redevelopment areas.
AI\IALYSIS
Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
The Commission has developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an
individual has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)
1. Is the requestor a public official?
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section
87100.) Councilmember Ira is a public official as defined by the Act. (Section 82048.)
2. Are the official's activities governed by the Act?
The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or
attempting to use one's official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section
87100.) These terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By deliberating and
voting on the proposed theatre project, Councilmember ka will be engaging in conduct
covered by the Act.
3. What are the official's economic interests?
A public official has a disqualiffing financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official,3 or on the
following enumerated economic interests:
3 For purposes of Section 87 1 03, a de cision will have a financial effect "on the official" if the
decision affects his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate
family. (Reg. 18703.5.) This is known as the personal financial effect rule. The rule does not appear to be
applicable to your facts.


Our File No. A-00-148
Page No. 3
1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 871030).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).
Councilmember ka is a parfirer in an accounting firm. As a partner, he has a 10
percent interest or more in the firm. Under the l0-percent, pass-through rule,
Councilmember Ira has an economic interest in a pro-rata share of income received by
the firm. (Section 82030.) Therefore, he will have an economic interest in a client of the
firm if his pro rata share of income from that client is $250 or more within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
According to your facts, Councilmember ha has an economic interest in a client
that is a small partnership, which owns and leases commercial property in the
redevelopment area.
Once a public official identifies all relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of those economic interests. This determination takes
three steps, which we discuss as Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine
whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision.
(Reg. 18700(b)(4).) Based upon the type of involvement, the official must then find the
applicable materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(bX5).)
After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it
is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. @eg. 18700(bX6).)
4. Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the
person initiates, is a named party in, or the subject of the decision. @eg. l870al@)(z).)
A person is the "subject of the decision" if the decision involves a license, permit, or
contract with that person. (Ibid.) If a person is not directly involved in the decision, then
the person is indirectly involved. (Reg. 18704.1(b).)
Councilmember Ira's client is indirectly involved in the city's decision to approve
a project located near the commercial property owned by the client.


Our File No. 4-00-148
Page No. 4
5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
Commission Regulation 18705.1 sets forth the materiality standard for sources of
income that are business entities, and that are indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. (Reg. 18705.30)(1).) The standards vary depending upon the size of the
business entity. The bigger the business, the greater the monetary impact must be for the
effect to be material.
For small business entities, such as Councilmember Ira's client, the applicable
materiality standard is Regulation 18705.1(bX7). Under that regulation, the eifect of a
decision is material if it will increase or decrease an company's: (1) gross revenues by
$10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2) expenses by $2,500 in a fiscal year; or (3) value of
assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
A financial effect is reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is
made if it is substantially likely that it will occur. (Regulation 13706.) A material
financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more than
a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FppC Ops. 198.)
Consequently, the question is whether it is substantially likely that the decision to
approve the proposed theatre complex will have a material financial effect on the
councilmember's client. Specifically, Councilmember ha must consider whether it is
substantially likely that the decision will affect his client's: (1) annual gross revenues by
$10,000 or more; (2) annual expenses by $2,500 or more; or (3) fair market value of real
property or other assets, or liabilities by $10,000 or more. @eg. lg705.lOX7).)
The purpose of any redevelopment plan is to promote sound development and
redevelopment of blighted areas. The anticipated result of redevelopment ii an increase
in property values within the project area. The very nature of redevilopment projects has
led the Commission to find that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be iome
financial effect on real property values and business interests located within or near
project areas. (In re oglesby (1975) I FPPC ops. 7l; Downey Cares v. Downey
Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983.) Moreover, Councilmember
Ira believes that it is reasonably foreseeable that the redevelopment decision will affect
the fair market value of his client's property by $10,000 or more. Therefore,
Councilmember Ira has a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the project.
7. Does the public generally exception apply?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate in the decision if the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from its effect


Our File No. 4-00-148
Page No. 5
on the public generally. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "public generally"
exception. For this exception to apply, the decision must affect the official in
substantially the silme manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public.
(Reg. 18707.) The jurisdiction of the city's redevelopment agency is the city.a
To determine whether the public generally exception applies here,
Councilmember ka must consider whether the decision will affect 50 percent of the
businesses in Redwood City in substantially the same manner as his client.
8. Does the Legally Required Participation RuIe apply?
The eighth step is the "legally required participation" rule. (Reg. lg70g.) This
rule only applies when several public officials in the same agency have a conflict of
interest in the same decision. The rule does not appear to be relevant to your facts.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
a
,/,\-
Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:JB:kw
o Under the Act, a local governmental agency's jurisdiction is the geographical area in which the
agency has jurisdiction. (Section 82035.) The jurisdiction of a city redevelop*".ri ugercy is the city.
(Health & Safety Code g 33120.)

from the site of a proposed theatre complex. As a member of the city's redevelopment
agency, does he have a conflict-of-interest in the decision to approve the project?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial on
commercial property owned by Councilmember Ira's client.
FACTS2
Councilmember Ira is an accountant and business partner in an accounting firm
known as C.G. Uhlenberg & Company,LLC. As a partner, Councilmember Ira has more
than a 10 percent interest in the accounting firm. One of his clients, a small partnership,
owns and leases a cofilmercial building in the city's redevelopment area. The

conversations with Councilmember Ira.


Our Page No. 2
commercial building is across the street from the site of a proposed theatre complex,

The city has five redevelopment areas.
